coredata.dc:title: Size-controlled synthesis of fluorescent tungsten oxide quantum dots via one-pot ethanol-thermal strategy for ferric ions detection and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhan, Yan
Liu, Yingliang
Liu, Qingqing
Liu, Zhiming
Yang, Haiyao
Lei, Bingfu
Zhuang, Jianle
Hu, Chaofan
coredata.dc:description: As one typical semiconductor material, tungsten oxide (WO3) has captivated intensive attention owing to its multifunctional properties. However, exploring a facile and one-pot strategy for controllable synthesis of fluorescent tungsten oxide quantum dots (WO3 QDs) is still very challenging. In this paper, WO3 QDs were prepared via a facile and one-pot ethanol-thermal approach in the presence of H2O2, and the growth mechanism was investigated in detail. Encouragingly, as evidenced by the transmission electron microscope and the atomic force microscope, the average size and topographic height of WO3 QDs were adjusted ranging from 23.5 to 2.3nm and 21.3 to 2.2nm via ethanol-thermal treatment with different amount of H2O2. The as-prepared WO3 QDs exhibited excellent water-solubility, highly fluorescence stability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, resulting in their successful applications in specific ferric ions detection and bioimaging in vitro. Furthermore, this significant strategy would pave the way for the controllable fabrication of other transition metal oxides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tungsten oxide quantum dots
Size-controlled synthesis
Ferric ions detection
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of WO3 QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the sized-controlled synthesis of WO3 QDs via one-pot ethanol-thermal treatment with H2O2.

(a–d) Typical TEM images, (e-h) size distributions and (i–l) AFM images of WO3 QDs prepared with 0.1, 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6mL H2O2 respectively. Insets of (a–d) and (i–l) represent the corresponding HRTEM images and height profiles performed along the white line, respectively.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) FTIR spectra, (c) XPS W 4f spectra and (d) O 1s spectra of the as-synthesized WO3 QDs. Inset of (a) shows the freeze-dried powder of WO3 QDs.

(a–d) UV–vis absorption, (e-h) excitation and PL spectra, (i–l) PL spectra of WO3 prepared with 0.1, 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6mL H2O2 respectively. Insets of (a-d) show the corresponding photographs under visible and 365nm UV light.

(a) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of WO3 QDs in the presence of Fe3+, (b) Relative fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs with different metal ions at the concentration of 1000μΜ, (c) Relative fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs in the presence of 1000μΜ different metal ions before (blank bars) and after (red bars) treatment with Fe3+, (d) PL spectra of WO3 QDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 1000μΜ, (e) The fluorescence recovery factor (I0-I)/I0 against the Fe3+ concentration. Inset: The liner relationship between the fluorescence recovery factor (I0-I)/I0 and Fe3+ concentration within 0–100μΜ. I0 represents the fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs without metal ions at 380nm excitation; I refers to the fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs with metal ions at 380nm excitation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Bright-filed microphotographs of HepG2 cells incubated with 100μg/mL WO3 QDs, (b), (c) and (d) Confocal laser scanning microphotographs of HepG2 cells recorded at excitation of 405, 488 and 561nm respectively.

head.ce:title: Size-controlled synthesis of fluorescent tungsten oxide quantum dots via one-pot ethanol-thermal strategy for ferric ions detection and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanics, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one typical semiconductor material, tungsten oxide (WO3) has captivated intensive attention owing to its multifunctional properties. However, exploring a facile and one-pot strategy for controllable synthesis of fluorescent tungsten oxide quantum dots (WO3 QDs) is still very challenging. In this paper, WO3 QDs were prepared via a facile and one-pot ethanol-thermal approach in the presence of H2O2, and the growth mechanism was investigated in detail. Encouragingly, as evidenced by the transmission electron microscope and the atomic force microscope, the average size and topographic height of WO3 QDs were adjusted ranging from 23.5 to 2.3nm and 21.3 to 2.2nm via ethanol-thermal treatment with different amount of H2O2. The as-prepared WO3 QDs exhibited excellent water-solubility, highly fluorescence stability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, resulting in their successful applications in specific ferric ions detection and bioimaging in vitro. Furthermore, this significant strategy would pave the way for the controllable fabrication of other transition metal oxides.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials, such as graphene, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), transition metal oxides (TMOs) and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), have aroused considerable interests due to their widespread applications [1–3]. As one kind of 2D TMOs, tungsten oxide, an n-type indirect semiconductor with tunable band gap of 2.5–2.8eV [4], is currently explored as a promising alternative to other conventional semiconductor materials, and these alluring physical properties and high specific surface areas make it a brand-new candidate for photocatalyst [5], supercapacitors [6–8], electrochromic devices [6,9,10], photothermal therapy [11,12] and gas sensors [13–15]. Owing to various phases and compositions, tungsten oxide can be classified into stoichiometric (WO3 and WO2) and non-stoichiometric (W5O14, W18O49, W20O68, W24O68, etc.) species [4]. It is universally known that the properties of material is determined by their microstructure and growth form, and materials in nanostructure exhibit unique performances that do not exist in their bulk form, which induce tremendous efforts to obtain tungsten oxide nanomaterials [16,17].

Over the past decade, many investigators have been predominately focusing on the non-stoichiometric nanostructure of tungsten oxide [18], for WO3, anisotropic structures of one- or two-dimensional nanomateris, such as nanowires or nanosheets have been prepared successfully [19,20], however, very little data is available on the preparation of WO3 QDs [13,21]. Compared to their one- or two-dimensional nanomaterials, zero-dimensional WO3 QDs provide enhanced optical and electronic properties resulting from quantum confinement effect when their sizes are down to 10nm [22,23]. With monodisperse particle sizes, excellent fluorescence performances, good biocompatibility and uniquely fluorescence quenched by Fe3+, zero-dimensional WO3 QDs are employed as a potential candidate for specific ferric ion detection and bioimaging. Although great progress in synthesis of one- or two-dimensional stoichiometric WO3 nanomaterials has been achieved, more or less drawbacks including introduction of other hetero atoms, poor quality or inadequate active sites can be found in prepared materials, and two key aspects related to the relatively few paper regarding preparing WO3 QDs are the complicated synthesis method and the incomplete optimization or understanding on the propriety of WO3 QDs [24]. Generally, the extraction of WO3 QDs can be divided into two approaches: bottom-up and top-down method [22,25]. For bottom-up method, Li et al. synthesized WO3 QDs in ethylene glycol solvent via microwave reaction using tungsten chloride (WCl6) as sources [21]. Monoclinic WO3 QDs were prepared by Morante et al. using W choroalkoxide in oleic acid through solvothermal processing [13]. For top-down method, two-pot approaches including sonochemical procedure and hydrothermal method were employed to obtain WO3-x QDs by Liu et al. using tungsten disulfide (WS2) as the precursor [26]. Xi et al. synthesized WO3-x QDs via ethanol-thermal treatment of WCl6 and hazardous chemical hydrazine hydrate (N2H4 .H2O) after keeping in a nitrogen-filled glove box until the mixing solution turned green [27]. Despite successful synthesis of WO3 QDs by reported approaches, organic solvent/template, intricate course, hazardous chemicals, harsh conditions or expensive devices may perplex the synthesis process or introduce other hetero atoms, thus influencing the properties of WO3 QDs and restricting their potential applications. Furthermore, much less efforts or investigations have been devoted on how the synthesis parameters are adjusted to controllably prepare WO3 QDs, and then effectively optimize the spectral properties. Consequently, it is still very challenging to develop a facile and environment-friendly synthesis approach of size-controlled WO3 QDs with excellent performances.

In this work, a green and one-pot strategy is firstly proposed for preparing WO3 QDs with tunable size, in which the precursor WS2 and the oxidizing species hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were processed via ethanol-thermal treatment, and there was no template, harsh conditions or hazardous chemical, effectively overcoming the difficulties related to previous reports. The as-obtained WO3 QDs exhibited excellent water-solubility, significant fluorescence stability, low toxicity and desirable biocompatibility, providing the primary superiority of promising application in living system. To explore the generation mechanism, we contrasted the photoluminescence of WO3 QDs processed in ethanol and water solvent, meanwhile, the function of H2O2 and ethanol solvent were discussed through comparative experiments. The effect of synthesis parameters on the photoluminescence of WO3 QDs was also been studied, compared with the synthesis time and temperature, the effect of the amount of H2O2 was more significant and regular. It is worth noting that the size distribution of WO3 QDs could be controlled by treating with different amount of H2O2 as evinced by the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and the atomic force microscope (AFM), and the spectral properties of WO3 QDs with different size distribution were investigated systematically. The quantum yield (QY) of WO3 QDs prepared via ethanol-treatment with 0.1, 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6mL H2O2 was estimated to be 2.15%, 3.28%, 35.68% and 35.26%, respectively, using quinine sulfate as a standard. Furthermore, based on these fascinating performances, it will be interesting to apply WO3 QDs in specific tasks, as exemplified here used as a fluorescence sensor for ferric iron ions and bioimaging in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of WO3 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Size-controlled WO3 QDs were synthesized via one-pot ethanol-thermal treatment in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 1 , the hydrophobic tungsten precursor WS2 dispersed in ethanol can be more interactive with H2O2, under high temperature and pressure, bulk WS2 powder is easily oxidized into fluorescent WO3 QDs. Without using any organic solvent/template or hazardous chemicals provides the superiority of facile and safe procedure. To investigate the influence of each synthesis parameter in this system, WO3 QDs prepared with different amount of H2O2 (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6mL H2O2) at 180°C for 20h were firstly explored, as shown in Fig. S1a, the fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs increases with the addition of 0.1, 0.2, 0.4 and 0.8mL H2O2, after that, it decreases with the addition of 1.6mL H2O2. Subsequently, WO3 QDs with 0.8mL H2O2 at different synthesis temperature (60, 90, 120, 150 and 180°C) for 20h were employed to study the influence of temperature, the results in Fig. S1b indicate that with rising of temperature, the fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs increases. Finally, WO3 QDs with 0.8mL H2O2 at 180°C for different synthesis time (4, 8, 12, 16, 20 and 24h) were synthesized to investigate the influence of synthesis time, Fig. S1c shows that WO3 QDs synthesized for 20h displays the maximum fluorescence intensity. All the above analysis reveal that WO3 QDs prepared with 0.8mL H2O2, at 180°C for 20h exhibit the most bright fluorescence. In addition, compared to the synthesis time and temperature, the influence of H2O2 on the fluorescence intensity is more evident and regular. Therefore, the properties of WO3 QDs prepared with different amount of H2O2 were later investigated systematically.

Fig. 2 shows the morphologies of WO3 QDs, interestingly, the size and height decrease along with the increase of H2O2. Fig. 2a and b reveals that the size distribution of WO3 QDs prepared with 0.1 and 0.4mL H2O2 is nonuninform, and the average size is 23.5 and 11.5nm (Fig. 2e–f). However, after ethanol-thermal treatment with 0.8 and 1.6mL H2O2, the size distribution of WO3 QDs is narrower and more uniform with average diameter 2.3 and 3.7nm (Fig. 2c–d and g–h). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images present the lattice spacing of all samples is 0.26nm (insets of Fig. 2a–d), corresponding to the (022) lattice fringe of WO3 [6,26]. Accordingly, the AFM analysis is similar to that of TEM, as shown in insets of Fig. 2i–l, the average topographic heights of the as-obtained WO3 QDs are 21.3, 5.6, 2.2 and 3.2nm subsequent to the ethanol-thermal treatment with 0.1, 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6mL H2O2, respectively. These above studies suggest that tunable synthesis of WO3 QDs can be achieved via one-pot ethanol-thermal treatment with different amount of H2O2.

To further identify the crystalline structure, the surface bonding information and chemical composition, the WO3 QDs prepared under optimal conditions were characterized with X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 a, all the presenting diffraction peaks are in good agreement with (111), (002), (200), (112), (121) and (022) planes of monoclinic WO3 (JCPDC 43–1035), a broad peak presented at 23° may be attributed to the influence of underlayer [19] and the weak peaks of WO3 QDs results from the small sizes and thin structures [28,29]. The WO3 QDs powder after freeze-drying is shown in the inset of Fig. 3a, which displays pale yellow color. The FTIR spectroscopy shown in Fig. 3b reveals that the absorption bands at around 3403 and 1601cm−1 may be assigned to the OH stretching vibration [30,31], endowing WO3 QDs with high water-solubility, and the bending vibration of COC at absorption band 1082cm−1 may result from the residual organic molecules [32,33]. In addition, the vibration in the region between 600 and 1000cm−1 correspond to the typical bending vibration of W-O [34,35], which confirms the generation of WO3 nanostructures. XPS measurements were further employed to gain chemical composition and chemical bonding of WO3 QDs. The W 4f spectrum in Fig. 3c presents two peaks centered at 37.3eV and 35.2eV represent W6+ states in the as-prepared WO3 QDs [36,37]. While the only one peak in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) can be deconvoluted into two peaks presented at 532.1eV and 530.8eV, which may be attributed to CO [38] and WO [6,39], corresponding to the previous reports. The XPS analysis results again indicate that oxygen-rich groups on the surface of WO3 QDs, such modification may greatly facilitate their aqueous-based applications.

For the proposed formation mechanism, the effects of solvent and H2O2 were investigated in detail. Zhang et al. have prepared MoOx QDs via one hydrothermal approach in the presence of H2O2 and KOH, using MoS2 as sources [40]. Liu et al. have reported one ethanol-thermal treatment of WCl6 for simultaneous synthesis of WO3-x QDs and nanowires without using H2O2 [24]. Based on the previous works, WS2 dispersed in ethanol and aqueous solution in the presence of H2O2 were employed to study the influence of solvent, the results in Fig. S2a are suggestive of the highly stronger PL intensity of WO3 QDs prepared via ethanol-thermal strategy, which may result from the reason that the ethanol solvent can restrict the fast decomposition of H2O2. Subsequently, the effect of H2O2 was investigated by comparing with the PL intensity of WO3 QDs prepared via ethanol-thermal strategy, from Fig. S2a, it can be clearly observed that WO3 QDs prepared via ethanol-thermal strategy without H2O2 shows negligible PL. Moreover, no generation of other fluorescent nanomaterials is further confirmed by the weaker fluorescence of products via ethanol-thermal treatment with different amount of H2O2 in the absence of WS2 (Fig. S2b). These above results reveal that the ethanol solvent and H2O2 are vital factors for the formation of fluorescent WO3 QDs. In this synthesis process, H2O2 in the ethanol solvent act as a sufficient oxygen supplier [41], the W4+ and S2− states are oxidized and exfoliated into W6+ and S6+ states, result in the formation of WO3 and SO4 2−, as shown in Fig. 1, except for the synthesis of WO3 QDs, H2SO4 and H2O are simultaneously obtained, and the explanation for the ethanol-thermal treatment with H2O2 is shown below [40,42]:
2WS2 +18H2O2 =2WO3 +4H2SO4 +14H2O
The generation of H2SO4 was proved by adding BaCl2 into the mixed solution after ethanol-thermal treatment with H2O2 (Fig. S2c).

Owing to the distinctive spectral properties, WO3 QDs prepared under optimal conditions were employed to be a fluorescent prober for metal ions detection and bio-imaging agent. Ferric ions are indispensable integrant in biological system, more or less ferric ions may be harmful to human body [44–46], exploring one sensitive and selective detection of ferric ions is of paramount significance. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence of WO3 QDs can be quenched with the addition of Fe3+, which may attributed to the electrostatic interaction of the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of WO3 QDs and Fe3+, resulting in the non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation via the effective electron transfer [24,47]. Two comparative experiments were measured to assess the selectivity of WO3 QDs for detecting Fe3+. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescence quenching of WO3 QDs mixed with 1000μm/L (μΜ) Fe3+ is calculated to be 79.9%, however, other metal ions including Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, K+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni+ and Zn2+ at the same concentration exert negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs. Moreover, the influences on the fluorescence quenching of WO3 QDs mixed with the mixture of 1000μΜ Fe3+ and 1000μΜ the aforementioned other metal ions are same to that of WO3 QDs mixed with 1000μΜ Fe3+ alone (Fig. 5c). Fig. 5d and e present the relation between the fluorescence quenching of WO3 QDs and the concentration of Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of WO3 QDs is gradually decreased in pace with the increase of Fe3+ varied from 0 to 1000μΜ, and the good liner regression equation is y=0.00248x+0.01075 (R2
=0.9962) with the concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 10 to 100μΜ. In addition, the influence pH and temperature on the fluorescence quenching of WO3 QDs with 100μΜ Fe3+ were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. S5a, the WO3 QDs solution in the presence of Fe3+ exhibits stronger PL intensity under acidic conditions, while the PL intensity under alkaline conditions decreases drastically, indicating the alkaline conditions have stronger influence on the fluorescence quenching of WO3 QDs in the presence of Fe3+. Fig. S5b shows the temperature-dependent PL emission spectra of WO3 QDs aqueous solution in the presence of Fe3+ recorded in the temperature range of 30–90°C. The PL intensity of WO3 QDs decreases as the temperature increase, revealing the thermal fluorescence quenching properties of the WO3 QDs in the presence of Fe3+. Remarkably, the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 2.69μΜ according to the signal to noise ratio of 3 (S/N=3), which is much lower than other fluorescent nano-probes (Table S1). It can be seen that the fluorescent WO3 QDs exhibit excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection.

To further extend the optical applications, the as-synthesized WO3 QDs were tested in HepG2 cells for bioimaging. The microphotographs of HepG2 cells were recorded with a laser scanning confocal microscope at excitation of 405, 488 and 561nm followed by being incubated with 100μg/mL WO3 QDs aqueous solution for 5h at 37°C and 5% CO2. Fig. 6 a displays the bright-field microphotographs of HepG2 cells. As illustrated in Fig. 6b–d, the HepG2 cells treated with WO3 QDs exhibit bright blue, green and red fluorescence at 405, 488 and 561nm excitation, and the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged for 2h irradiation, suggesting that the ultrathin WO3 QDs can be internalized into cells as a promising biomarker for bio-imaging due to their small size [48,49]. Furthermore, the HepG2 cells incubated with WO3 QDs do not wreck their normal morphology and cellular activity.

The cytotoxicity of WO3 QDs was examined by MTT assay. A dose and time-dependent experiment was employed to assess the toxicity of WO3 QDs, in which HepG2 cells incubated in DMEM with different concentrations of WO3 QDs ranging from 0 to 100μg/mL for 24 and 48h respectively. The relative viability of cells shown in Fig. S5 indicates that the WO3 QDs impose low or negligible toxicity on HepG2 cells. Therefore, with high water-solubility, excellent spectral properties, low toxicity as well as good biocompatibility, the as-synthesized WO3 QDs have enormous potential in biomedical applications.

Commercial bulk tungsten disulfide (WS2) was obtained from Aladdin Regent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (99.7%) and H2O2 (30%) were purchased from Guangzhou Jinhuada Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Guangzhou, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl), lithium chloride (LiCl), ferric chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), aluminium chloride (AlCl3), Magnesium chloride (MgCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), chromium chloride (CrCl3), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4
. 5H2O), silver sulfate (Ag2SO4) and Mercuric nitrate (Hg(NO3)2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2, 5-dipheny-ltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from GIBCO (Grand Island, NY, United States). All the reagents were of AR grade, and the ultrapure water was produced with a Milli-Q system.

Size-controlled WO3 QDs were synthesized via one-pot ethanol-thermal treatment with different amount of H2O2. Detailly, 30mg WS2 was dispersed in 45mL ethanol, and then added 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6mL H2O2. After stirred for 15min, the mixture was transferred in to a Teflon-sealed autoclave at 200°C for 18h. Cooling down to room temperature, the resulted yellow suspension of WO3 QDs was vacuum-filtrated using 0.22μm pores cellulose ester membrane. Subsequently, the suspension was collected to freeze-dry followed by dialyzing against to ultrapure water for 48h. The WO3 QDs powder was prepared and redispersed in water for further study.

HRTEM images were taken with a JEOL JEM-2010 microcopy at 200 Kv (Japan). AFM images were taken on a SPM Dimension 3100 microscope from Veeco (USA). The XRD patterns were performed with a XD-2X/M4600 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (λ=1.54051Å) (China). The FTIR spectrum was conducted on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (USA). The XPS was recorded with a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a Hitachi UV-2550 UV–vis spectrometer (Japan). The PL spectroscopy was obtained with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan). The confocal fluorescence microscope was measured with a Zeiss LSM 710 confocal laser scanning microscope (Germany).

The quantum yield (QY) of WO3 QDs was estimated according to the established procedure. Typically, as the standard, the quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY: 0.6 at 280 and 320nm, and 0.54 at 380 and 420nm) [50,51]. UV–vis absorption and PL spectroscopy (with 280, 320, 380 and 420nm excitation) were examined respectively. The QY of WO3 QDs was calculated using the question below
ϕ X
=
ϕ
S T
(
G r a
d X
G r a
d
S T
)
(
η X 2
η
S T
2
)
Where the subscripts “ST” and “X” refer to the standard and test respectively, “Φ” represents the quantum yield, “Grad” denotes the slope from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and “η” represents the refractive index of the solvent.

Liver human hepatoma (HepG2) cells maintained in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin were incubated in 96-well plates (5×103 cells per well) in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37°C for 24h. The fluorescent WO3 QDs suspension (100μg/mL) was passed through a 0.22μm sterile filter membrane, and then the HepG2 cells were cultured in the presence of filtered suspension mixed with 200mL DMEM for 5h. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with fresh PBS to remove excessive WO3 QDs, and the cellular image was taken with a laser scanning confocal microscope at 405, 488 and 561nm excitation wavelength. The viability of HepG2 cells was evaluated by MTT assays. After incubated for 24h, the HepG2 cells were treated different concentrations of WO3 QDs ranging from 0 to 100μg/mL for 24 and 48h respectively. Then the medium was replaced with a fresh medium containing MTT (20μL, 5mg/mL) in each well and the HepG2 cells were incubated for another 4h. Finally, the absorbance of each well was measured and optical density (OD) was recorded at 490nm to assess the relative viability of HepG2 cells. The cell viability was determined by the following equation:
C e l l v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
O
D
T r e a t e d
O
D
C o n t r o l
× 100 %
Where the “ODTreated ” and “ODControl ” represent the optical density in the presence of WO3 QDs and in the absence of WO3 QDs.

In summary, fluorescent WO3 QDs with average size ranging from 23.5 to 2.3nm were synthesized via one-pot ethanol-thermal treatment with different amount of H2O2. Without using organic solvent/template or hazardous chemicals provides the superiority of facile and safe procedure. Encouragingly, the spectral properties of WO3 QDs were adjusted with different size distributions, and the generation mechanism is discussed in detail. Meanwhile, synthesis parameters including the concentration of H2O2, synthesis time and temperature were investigated and optimized by comparing with PL intensity of WO3 QDs. Furthermore, the as-obtained WO3 QDs exhibited high aqueous dispersity, excellent fluorescence stability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, endowing them as a new but promising candidate for specific ferric ion detection and bioimaging. It is worth noting that, this synthesis strategy would open a new avenue for adjustable synthesis of other transition metal oxides as well as metal-free 2D nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51402207, 21571067, 51372091, 21401028, 61675072), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (201605030005), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2014A010105035, 2015B090903074).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

One-step hydrothermal synthesis of monolayer MoS2 quantum dots for highly efficient electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution
One-step preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots from oxidized debris of graphene oxide
A facile and one-pot synthesis of fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots for bio-imaging application
Facile surfactant-free synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4@3-aminophenol–formaldehyde core-shell magnetic microspheres
Extraction of graphitic carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal treatment commercially activated carbon: the role of cation–π interaction
Growth and assembly of crystalline tungsten oxide nanostructures assisted by bioligation
Study of the interaction between simple molecules and W-Sn based oxide catalysts. 2. The Case of W-Sn-O Mixed Oxide Powders
Characterization of zirconia-supported tungsten oxide catalyst
Combustion synthesis and characterization of nanocrystalline WO3
One-step catalase controllable degradation of C3N4 for N-doped carbon dot green fabrication and their bioimaging applications
Morphology-controlled synthesis of W18O49 nanostructures and their near-infrared absorption properties
Highly photoluminescent molybdenum oxide quantum dots: one-pot synthesis and application in 2 4, 6-trinitrotoluene determination
Spontaneous exfoliation and tailoring of MoS2 in mixed solvents
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Chemically tailoring graphene oxides into fluorescent nanosheets for Fe 3+ ion detection
Dual functional N-and S-co-doped carbon dots as the sensor for temperature and Fe 3+ ions
Facile green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots using Chionanthus retusus fruit extract and investigation of their suitability for metal ion sensing and biological applications
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg 2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
A tunable ratiometric pH sensor based on carbon nanodots for the quantitative measurement of the intracellular pH of whole cells
N-doped multi-fluorescent carbon dots for ‘turn off-on’silver-biothiol dual sensing and mammalian cell imaging application
2-Aminopyridine as a standard for low-wavelength spectrofluorimetry
Quantum efficiencies of fluorescence of organic substances: effect of solvent and concentration of the fluorescent solute

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yan Zhan received BS degree in Datong University in 2014. He is currently study for the MS degree in College of Mechanics, Taiyuan University of technology. His research interests include synthesis of two-dimensional nanomaterials and their applications toward the biology.

Yingliang Liu received his MS degree in 1989 from Changchun Institute Applied Chemistry of Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) and PhD degree in 1994 from Inorganic Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University. He has been working as professor at Jinan University since 2002 and moved from Jinan University to South China Agricultural University in 2012. His main research interests include the topics of luminescence materials and photoelectron materials.

Qingqing Liu received MS degree in South China of Normal University in 2010. She is currently working toward PhD degree in College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of technology. Her current research is focused on the synthesis of carbon-based nanoparticles and their applications.

Zhiming Liu received his MS degree in 2010 and PhD degree in 2013 respectively in College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University. He is currently a lecturer at South China Normal University and his main research is focused on the synthesis of biological nanomaterials.

Haiyao Yang received his BS degree in Tianjin University in 2015, he is a graduate student in College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University and his current research is the synthesis of optical nanomaterials and applications.

Bingfu Lei received MS degree in 2004 from Jinan University and PhD degree in 2007 from Condensed Matter Physics, Changchun Institute of Institute of Optical Fine Mechanics and Physics of Chinese Academy of Sciences. He obtained professor position in 2012 and his current research is the exploration of covalently grafted hybrid as chemosensors and gas sensors.

Jianle Zhuang received his MS degree in 2007 and PhD degree in 2009 from College of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University. He is currently an associate professor at South China Agricultural University. His main research fields concerned with functional nanomaterials and rare earth doped luminescent materials.

Chaofan Hu received his MS degree in 2010 from College of Biophotonics, South China of Normal University and PhD degree in 2013 from College of Chemistry, Jinan University. Currently, he is a lecturer at South China Agricultural University, his interests includes synthesis of optical biomaterials and their applications.
